ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle soup with chicken balls; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Thai boat noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Thai boat noodle soup with chicken balls.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai chicken ball boat noodle soup, approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, broth, chicken balls, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Boat noodle soup (Kuai Tiao Ruea) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodle soup with chicken meatballs and Thai-style broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai boat noodle soup with chicken balls exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated by component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked rice noodles (FDC 168936), chicken meatballs (approximated from ground chicken cooked, FDC 171477), and chicken broth (FDC 171155). Proportions based on a typical 400 g bowl: ~120 g rice noodles, ~80 g chicken balls, ~180 g broth, ~20 g vegetables and condiments. Per-100 g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles, boiled (GI 53)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai boat noodle soup with chicken balls. GI of 53 (low) applied from boiled rice noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source, per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 36.0 g) / 100 = 19.1. Available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 9.0 g/100 g × 4 = 36.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g bowl provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit condiment additions such as fish sauce, and consider a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The rice noodle base contributes a glycemic load of approximately 19 per serving (GI 53, low category), which is manageable within a diabetic meal plan when portion size is controlled and the dish is paired with adequate protein from chicken balls and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1280 mg per serving) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation relevant to cardiovascular disease; saturated fat is moderate at ~1.6 g per serving, and the lean chicken ball protein source is preferable to red meat alternatives, but sodium reduction remains the primary dietary modification recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Ball Boat Noodle Soup (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือลูกชิ้นไก่, romanized: Kuai Tiao Ruea Luk Chin Kai) is a Thai noodle soup dish inspired by the traditional boat noodles (Kuai Tiao Ruea) historically sold from canal boats in central Thailand, particularly in Ayutthaya and Bangkok. The dish consists of springy rice noodles served in a rich, dark, aromatic broth typically made from chicken or pork stock seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, dried chilli, sugar, and vinegar. The defining feature is the addition of bouncy, hand-formed chicken balls (luk chin kai) that provide a tender, juicy protein component. The soup is garnished with bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), fried garlic, and fresh herbs. The broth is characteristically bold, slightly sweet, sour, and savoury — a hallmark of Thai boat noodle flavour profiles. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is popular as a light yet satisfying meal. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 272 kcal, 20.8 g protein, 5.6 g fat, and 36 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively low in saturated fat but high in sodium (~1280 mg per serving) due to the seasoned broth and fish sauce, making sodium management the primary dietary consideration. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low GI), making it a moderate glycemic load meal suitable for most individuals when portion-controlled. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai chicken ball boat noodle soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component assembly from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked rice noodles, cooked ground chicken, and chicken broth in proportions representative of a standard bowl. GI is analogously estimated from boiled rice noodles per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no exact dish-level entry was found in any approved primary source. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis or an HPB-equivalent entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean chicken and rice noodle components per USDA data. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate; the full dish GI has not been directly measured. 7. Halal status is listed as false because halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation cannot be confirmed from available data; the dish may be halal-suitable depending on ingredient sourcing. 8. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record; however, this dish is a mixed noodle soup and could also be classified under 'Mixed' — the input classification is retained as the protein component (chicken balls) is the defining nutritional feature.
